WHAT IS DEPRESSION ?

The World Health Organization defines depression
as a mental disorder, characterized by sadness,
loss of interest or pleasure, feelings of
guilt or low self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbances, a feeling of fatigue and a
lack of concentration.

It therefore variably includes:
Almost constant sadness, sometimes with crying
Loss of interest and pleasure in activities
normally enjoyed
Feelings of worthlessness and guilt
Recurrent thoughts of death or suicide
Psychomotor slowing
Fatigue or loss of energy
Loss of appetite

WHAT CARE OPTIONS ARE POSSIBLE?
Eat a normal, varied diet
Correct anemia
Correct identified deficiencies
Assess (only with the practitioner) the relevance of reducing medication doses, stopping them, or switching them

Alongside specific treatments such as anxiolytics or antidepressants, whose prescription should be discussed with the referring practitioner and, if possible, a psychiatrist, psychotherapeutic interventions are possible.
Specifically address psychological problems (health coaching, drug treatments, psychotherapy)
Avoid social isolation: limit hospitalizations, keep working, go out, love.

It has been shown that brief solution-focused therapy is effective for fatigue associated with IBD.

FATIGUE

AND MICI

PREVENTION

#06

WHAT IS FATIGUE?

It is difficult to give a definition of fatigue but, in a pragmatic way, one can suggest that it is a complaint resulting from an imbalance between what must be accomplished and what can be done. We distinguish physiological fatigues after physical exertion or from lack of sleep, for example, which are improved by rest, and pathological fatigues that are little improved by rest or sleep. The latter can be somatic, that is related to an identified pathology, or psychological in the context of anxiety or depressive syndromes. In the context of MICI, somatic fatigue and psychological fatigue are often mixed.

WHAT ROLE DOES FATIGUE PLAY IN MICI?

Fatigue is extremely common in MICI since it is reported in 44-86% of cases during flares, and this fatigue persists in 22-41% of patients during remission. It is frequently associated with sleep disturbances and negatively impacts quality of life, which is now a criterion evaluated in most studies concerning MICI.

In the French BIRD study, carried out on 1,185 French patients and published in February 2017, severe fatigue was found in nearly 50% of them; this fatigue persisted in half of the cases in remission.
A significant decrease in quality of life was observed in 53% of patients.

Gut inflammation (visible on endoscopy, by measuring calprotectin in the stool, or on MRI) and systemic inflammation (visible on blood tests, especially with C-reactive protein) are markers of IBD activity and severity. It is mainly present during flare-ups but can persist at baseline in patients in remission. There are also so-called "chronic active" forms where symptoms are minimal (or, in any case, tolerated by patients) but where intestinal lesions and therefore inflammation persist.

This chronic inflammation could contribute to fatigue through the action of certain pro-inflammatory proteins produced in excess during IBD and thus partly explain why more than 80% of IBD patients say they feel fatigued during an inflammatory flare. But a recent study looked at patients in remission by comparing a "fatigued" group and a "non-fatigued" group. In the "fatigued" group, there is notably an excess production of TNFα and interferon γ. This might perhaps link the phenomenon of fatigue (with all its subjectivity) to quantifiable biological factors. These findings nevertheless need to be confirmed.

WHAT ARE THE MOST FREQUENT RISK FACTORS?

Several studies show that fatigue is more frequent in women, in patients who are unemployed or who have financial difficulties, in cases of low socio-educational level, in smokers, with a recent diagnosis, in active disease and in anemia. There is, however, no difference according to patients' age and almost no difference between Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis.

WHAT ARE THE
MAIN CAUSES
OF ANEMIA?

Anemia is very common in IBD, especially during the first
year after diagnosis (about 2/3 of patients) and its causes are numerous:
= Acute bleeding (rapid drop in hemoglobin) or chronic (progressive drop in hemoglobin due to chronic iron loss) during flares or in poorly controlled disease (so-called chronic active forms)

#O7

AND DEFICIENCIES

WHAT IS A DEFICIENCY?

A deficiency is the lack of an important element
for the proper functioning of the body. We
will address deficiencies in vitamins, iron and trace
elements. Fatigue, but also shortness of breath (particularly
with exertion) or a faster heart rate are clearly among the classic symptoms of anemia,
whereas there is no clear scientific proof that a vitamin
deficiency alone is a sufficient cause of fatigue. One can nevertheless think pragmatically
that it is better not to have deficiencies in order to feel well.

WHAT IS ANEMIA?

By definition, this is a decrease in the serum level of hemoglobin, a protein contained in red blood cells that carries part of the body's iron (65%) and whose essential role is to bring oxygen from the lungs to the organs. A level < 12 g/dL for men and < 11 g/dL for women is considered to indicate anemia. In the majority of cases this is accompanied by a decrease in the number of red blood cells.

Iron deficiency is defined almost exclusively
by the serum ferritin level, which varies according to
general inflammation. It is accepted that levels should be
maintained > 30 µg/L in the absence of serum inflammation and >
100 µg/L in case of inflammation. It is estimated that 45% of
IBD patients suffer from iron deficiency (notably
during the first year after diagnosis).

Can simple iron deficiency without anemia
cause fatigue? This debate is not definitively
settled. Research in IBD does not allow
us to affirm this, but a recent meta-analysis (artificial pooling
of several studies to improve statistical power) conducted in
non-IBD patients tends to prove it for the first time.

ABSENCES FOR
MEDICAL CARE

When you have UC or Crohn's disease, you may need to be absent
from work for consultations, tests or treatments (infusions in particular) and this
can be difficult to manage with your employer. What can you do?

GENERAL CASE

Your employer cannot refuse to allow you
to attend your medical appointments, provided
that you inform them in advance (except in an
emergency, of course). However, they are not legally
required to pay you for the time spent at these
appointments.

Some employers are understanding;
start by explaining your situation to your manager or
to an HR representative; you may be able to arrange
to have your appointments during working time.

Also check whether the status of disabled worker
(RQTH) leads to accommodations
in these situations (this depends on companies
and collective agreements, so it is not
systematic). If this is the case, do not hesitate to
request the RÔTH (see the afa fact sheet on the subject)
If the employer refuses to let you be absent
during working hours, you will have to take
leave or RTT to attend your consultations,
treatments or tests, or arrange with the employer the
making up of work hours not completed.
Some patients prefer to systematically
take leave, so as not to have to disclose their
health condition to their employer: it is up to you to decide
according to your situation.

IN DAY HOSPITAL

Infusions for treatments or for iron,
coloscopies … are performed as day hospitalizations. In this case the hospital gives you a situation form, which has the same value as a sick leave certificate.
You must forward it to your employer and possibly
to social security as with a usual sick leave (see your HR department if you have
any doubts about the procedure to follow).

You will therefore be on sick leave for that day (sick leave is not split into half-days) and, depending on your entitlements and your situation, you may receive daily sickness benefits from social security.
For people with ALD, the 3-day waiting period for the payment of daily benefits applies only once per 3-year period.
During sick leave the employer may pay a top-up to your salary; the rules depend on your length of service in the company (generally at least one year) and on the applicable collective bargaining agreement.
The same applies to the rules for taking waiting days into account.

FACT SHEET

#09

AND TREATMENTS

IMPACT OF CORTICOSTEROIDS
ON FATIGUE

Corticosteroids usually have an initial stimulating effect that combats fatigue. But in cases of prolonged use or when adrenal insufficiency is revealed upon their discontinuation, the phenomenon is reversed with marked fatigue often associated with muscle pains that reinforce this feeling of tiredness.

IMPACT OF
IMMUNOSUPPRESSANTS ON
FATIGUE

Similarly, azathioprine and Purinethol can cause fatigue at the start of treatment. But this effect disappears in the vast majority of cases within a few weeks, generally not requiring stopping the treatment. It is important to warn the patient of this and thus help them get through the first weeks. This fatigue is sometimes associated with fairly bothersome muscle pains which can, in some cases, lead to treatment interruption.

This is a cause frequently reported by patients and that practitioners sometimes find hard to hear. It is nevertheless clear that some medications can favour fatigue. However, these medications are often essential to the treatment.

Methotrexate is a medication that can cause fatigue in the hours and sometimes days following its administration (by intramuscular, subcutaneous, or oral route) in a non-negligible percentage of patients. Unlike azathioprine or Purinethol, this effect does not always lessen over time and may require discontinuation of the treatment. Nevertheless, this decision can only be made in agreement with the responsible practitioner and must be weighed against the risks of disease worsening.

IMPACT OF ANTI-TNE
ON FATIGUE

Numerous studies show that anti-TNFα agents significantly improve fatigue scores and quality of life scores. There are also some conflicting data reporting an excess of fatigue in patients treated with anti-TNFα, but this may be related to more severe and/or more active disease.

TREATMENTS

FERTILITY - CONCEPTION
The fertility of patients with inactive IBD and no prior surgical history is the same as that of women without IBD. Conception should ideally be considered during a period of disease quiescence so that the pregnancy proceeds as well as possible. Indeed, flares at the time of conception and during pregnancy increase the risks of growth retardation, of delivery

#14

PREGNANCY AND IBD

When you have IBD and are planning a pregnancy, it is normal to have questions! According to the "Crossed Perspectives on IBD" survey carried out by afa in 2020, one person in five reports having given up on the plan to have a child, even though many women with IBD manage to achieve it! This fact sheet informs you about fertility and whether to continue or stop your treatment in case of pregnancy or breastfeeding.

Treatments for the disease are most often continued at the time of conception and during pregnancy for this purpose.
The only surgery that impairs fertility is coloproctectomy with ileo-anal anastomosis by laparotomy, but this is rare and the laparoscopic approach used for several years has changed its impact on fertility.

You will find answers in this fact sheet! You must not stay alone with your questions:
talk about them with your parents or your doctor!

It is difficult to talk about a definitive cure because we do not yet know enough about the cause of this disease. Fortunately there are medications to calm flare-up periods (attacks), allow you to be in good shape and make abnormal signs disappear for long periods. It is possible that the disease will have relapses but, even in severe or complicated cases, the outcomes are ultimately good.

DOING AN INTERNSHIP OR STUDIES ABROAD WITH IBD

Doing an internship abroad or part of one’s studies is becoming increasingly common. This step can enhance one’s educational background but also represents a very enriching life experience, accessible to a young person with IBD provided the project is well prepared.
This information sheet does not address every situation but offers some avenues; it may be supplemented based on the experiences of our members (do not hesitate to contact us).

GENERAL INFORMATION

If the internship or study period is carried out as part of schooling at a French educational institution, the student remains registered at that institution and continues to benefit from French Social Security, and therefore from reimbursement for exams, consultations or medications carried out in France or purchased in France during the period concerned.

The main questions that arise during a study stay abroad are those of coverage for care or treatments provided locally.

IN EUROPE (when coverage in France is maintained)

+ For a stay in a country of the European Union, request before departure a European Health Insurance Card (in French: Carte Européenne de Sécurité Sociale), which allows medical care in the destination country through the public health system. Depending on the situation, you may not have to pay medical costs up front, or you may pay up front and be reimbursed either in the country of stay or in France (see the links below for more details by country). The application can be made online on the AMELI website.

+ The European Health Insurance Card can also be used in Iceland, Switzerland and Norway.

+ French prescriptions are accepted in European countries (in English and with the generic names of the products).

Crohn's - UC: France

TOBACCO:
IT'S NOT FOR ME!

In this leaflet, we explain how tobacco affects your health and especially your Crohn's disease or your ulcerative colitis. The idea: don’t start smoking or stop quickly if you already smoke.

TOBACCO IS REALLY NOT A FRIEND!

Tobacco is the leading cause of preventable death. In France, 200 people die from it every day — that’s 75,000 per year, it’s enormous! It’s like a plane crash every day!

Tobacco contains more than 4,000 chemical substances, including toxic gases and heavy metals, which are very harmful and carcinogenic!

Among these substances is nicotine, which is the most addictive drug there is, ahead of heroin, alcohol and cannabis. When you smoke a cigarette, nicotine reaches the brain in just a few seconds. It releases dopamine, which gives a feeling of pleasure and well‑being, like when basic needs are satisfied such as eating, drinking, breathing, sleeping. The brain quickly gets used to this increase in dopamine and in re-

urge. That's what creates addiction.
Smoking then becomes a need like
eating, drinking, breathing, sleeping. You then
feel like you need to smoke to
feel good or to avoid feeling bad.

When you're a teenager, it's particularly
dangerous because your brain is
still developing. Nicotine can
affect the parts that manage attention,
learning, mood and impulse control.

There is also carbon monoxide:
it's an odorless, colorless gas that is
released in any combustion (it will
also be present if you smoke mint leaves
or cannabis...) and that binds
more easily to your red blood cells than
oxygen. Oxygen is the fuel for
all the cells in your body; it's what
allows them to function.

RIGHTS AND INSURANCE 05

AT WORK

TO TALK ABOUT IT OR NOT?

In a context where work is becoming both harder to obtain and more demanding, the question of mentioning one’s IBD remains a delicate subject. The majority of patients prefer not to talk about it, to be seen as "normal". But that is to deprive oneself of a source of support that can be valuable psychologically, and of course not to benefit from specific measures for access to employment and training or from workplace accommodations.
In any case, this remains an individual decision, which depends on the course of the disease and the constraints it imposes, on the perceived ability of your professional circle to accept this information and, finally, to draw positive consequences from it for you.
If you decide to talk about it, you need to be prepared: whom to tell, how, what

AM I REQUIRED TO TALK ABOUT MY ILLNESS AT WORK?

The professional environment, whatever it may be (your hierarchical superior, the human resources department) does not have the right to ask you personal questions. You therefore have no legal obligation to talk about your illness, which is part of your private life. No one will be able to reproach you later for not having disclosed it, for example at the time of hiring.

Even if you have Recognition of the Status of Disabled Worker (see Rights and insurance - Fact sheet No. 1 Being recognized as a disabled worker), you are not obliged to declare it. The Afa advises requesting it as a preventive measure, given the time required to obtain it in case the illness evolves.

The occupational physician is the only person in the company authorized to collect medical information. Informing them may help you obtain workplace accommodations if necessary. But again, there is no obligation.

“O3 HOSPITALIZATION

Practically all patients with Crohn’s disease or ulcerative colitis will, during the course of their illness, require hospitalization. Most often, it can — and should — be planned to avoid having to manage complications or problems at times when the only thing that should matter is preserving moral comfort in a situation of physical discomfort due to hospitalization.

Afa is available for any information: 0 811 091 623 (Monday to Friday from 2:00 pm to 6:00 pm) or info-accueil@afa.asso.fr
Santé Info Droits, the CISS legal and social helpline: 0 810 004 333 (Monday, Wednesday and Friday from 2:00 pm to 6:00 pm / Tuesday and Thursday from 2:00 pm to 8:00 pm)

The trusted person:
During any hospitalization in a health care facility, the patient is offered the opportunity to designate a trusted person. The trusted person can be a parent, a relative or the primary care physician. They will be consulted, for example, if the patient is unable to express their wishes and receive the necessary information. This designation is made in writing. This designation is valid for the duration of the hospitalization, unless the patient decides otherwise.

YOUR RIGHTS AND DUTIES IN THE HOSPITAL

Every user of the public hospital has a number of rights. These were significantly strengthened by the law of March 4, 2002, relating to patients' rights and the quality of the health system, as well as by the "Charter of the Hospitalized Person". These rights of the sick person (of the patient) range from the guarantee of quality care to access to information, including respect for intimacy and private life.

WHAT ARE THEY USED FOR ?
1 • To preserve longer: Some prevent foods from molding or spoiling too quickly (e.g.: E250 or sodium nitrite, notably used in ham).
2 • To improve color: They make foods more attractive (e.g.: E160b, which gives an orange color to chips).
3 • To give a better taste: Some additives enhance taste or smell (e.g.: E621, the glutamate found for example in savory snacks, instant noodles and many ready-made meals).
4 • To give a pleasant texture: To thicken a sauce, make a cake tender (e.g.: E440, pectin in jams).
WHAT IMPACT DO THEY HAVE ON OUR HEALTH ?
Some are harmless (like the pectin from fruit or citric acid from lemon), but others are controversial, either because studies have proven their negative impact on health or because they are suspected of having one.
For example, recent studies have shown that some sweeteners or sugar substitutes (e.g.: aspartame, E951) increase the risk of cancer. They are nevertheless widely used in drinks, sodas, desserts, chewing gum and

THE INGREDIENT LIST: WHO SAYS WHAT?
Ingredients are always listed from the most to the least present. If sugar or oil is at the top of the list, that isn’t necessarily a good choice, unless you’re looking at a bottle of oil or a bag of candy. For example, if you buy fruit juice and the first ingredient is water, it’s more of a fruit-based drink.
In general, you should understand all the ingredients listed. It’s a bit like following a recipe: you’re more likely to choose the ones where you know every ingredient.
Food additives (like E102, E150, etc.): the fewer there are, the better. There are 320 in use today. Some are harmless, but others are more questionable.
For people with IBD, emulsifiers are particularly problematic. (See the “Food additives” fact sheet in MIKADO to learn more and to help spot them.)

GOOD TO KNOW!
To help you choose, you can use the Open Food Facts app; it’s the Wikipedia of foods, free and collaborative.
HOW DOES IT WORK?
You scan a product’s barcode with your phone
The app analyzes its composition and gives you a score
It explains the rating criteria: health, environment
It even suggests alternatives that better match your original criteria
Sometimes certain scores are based on criteria that don’t take everything into account (example: Yuka penalizes cheese for its fats, even though it’s a natural product). But they are still super useful for decoding labels! Open Food Fact mentions the NOVA classification.
It’s a system that classifies foods according to their degree of processing:
GROUP 1 • Unprocessed or minimally processed foods These are the most natural foods: fruits, vegetables, eggs, milk, meat, rice… In short, everything that is close to its original state.
GROUP 2 • Processed culinary ingredients Oil, sugar, salt... These are ingredients we add for cooking, but that we don’t eat on their own.

WHAT EXACTLY IS IT?
The gut microbiota is a community of bacteria, viruses, parasites and fungi that are good for you and live in your intestine. They are essential for your body to work properly. And they alone make up between 1 and 3 kg of your weight!

WHY IS THE MICROBIOTA SO IMPORTANT?
Your microbiota plays several essential roles:
It helps digest certain foods that your digestive tract can't handle on its own.
It boosts your immune system and helps you fight microbes.
It influences your mood and your brain (because yes, your gut and your brain are connected!).
It helps you absorb the vitamins and minerals your body needs.

CROHN'S DISEASE, ULCERATIVE COLITIS: WHAT'S THE CONNECTION WITH THE MICROBIOTA?
Several researchers, supported by afa, are currently studying the microbiota and are interested in the role of our environment and our lifestyle on it. Indeed, each person has a microbiota that is unique. For example, it can depend on where they live, their diet, whether they exercise or not, and even whether they have a pet!

-this. Indeed, each person has a microbiota that is unique. Depending, for example, on where they live, their diet, whether they exercise or not, and even whether they have a pet!

In cases of Crohn’s disease or ulcerative colitis, the microbiota becomes unbalanced. This is called dysbiosis.

WHY? BECAUSE IN IBD:
Inflammation attacks the microbiota.
The balance of bacteria is broken, thus disrupting the immune function of the microbiota.
Chicken or egg, it’s hard to know which comes first. Is it the inflammation that disrupts the microbiota or the microbiota imbalance that causes inflammation?
Result: your body is tired, your belly hurts and your immune system gets out of balance.

THE INTESTINAL MICROBIOTA:
YOUR SECRET ARMY!
Imagine an army of billions of tiny creatures that live in your belly and work 24/7 to keep you healthy… Well, that’s your intestinal microbiota!

Introduction
IBD can be diagnosed throughout life. 
However, there are specific features unique to the pediatric
form. For example, Crohn disease is more often 
diagnosed at the inflammatory stage,

diagnosed throughout life.
However, there are specific features particular to the pediatric form.
For example, Crohn’s disease is more often diagnosed at the inflammatory stage, before the appearance of stricturing or fistulizing complications. Ulcerative colitis is frequently characterized by extensive colonic involvement at diagnosis. In pediatrics, IBDs are associated with growth (height and weight) and pubertal delay but also with lowered self-esteem, deterioration of body image and a high rate of school absenteeism, thereby compromising quality of life and future social and professional integration. The cost of managing these conditions in childhood exceeds that in adults (1). Better knowledge of the specificities of the pediatric forms is essential for all actors involved in medical and paramedical care.

Epidemiology of pediatric IBD
IBD mainly affect children after the age of 10, in the peri-pubertal period. They are rare before age 5 and exceptional before 1 year (less than 1% of pediatric cases). We distinguish "pediatric-onset" IBD that begin after 10 years, the "EO (early onset – IBD)" that begin before 10 years and the "VEO (very early onset) – IBD" that begin before 6 years.
Although genetics play a role in the pathogenesis of IBD at any age, monogenic etiologies are more frequently represented in patients presenting with VEO-IBD compared with forms diagnosed later. An increasing number of causal monogenic variants have been identified (and continue to be identified) in patients with VEO-IBD, particularly in infantile-onset forms (before age 2) (2-4). A significant proportion of the monogenic etiologies observed in VEO-IBD fall within primary immunodeficiencies (2,5,6).
To date, the monogenic etiologies identified in VEO-IBD can be classified into six major (sometimes overlapping) categories: immune deregulation

primary immune deficiencies (2,5,6).
To date, the monogenic etiologies identified in VEO-IBD can be classified into six major (sometimes overlapping) categories: general immune dysregulation, T- and B-lymphocyte deficiencies, abnormalities of phagocytic function, hyperinflammatory and auto-inflammatory syndromes, epithelial barrier dysfunction and other conditions (2,5,6). Although a proportion of patients with VEO-IBD have an underlying monogenic etiology, the majority of them (> 70–80%) do not have a specific genetic cause identified (7).
Worldwide, the incidence of pediatric IBD is highest in Canada, Northern Europe, and New Zealand. It is lowest in Southern Europe, Africa, Asia and South America (8). Among the 37 studies assessing incidence over time, 31 showed an increase in that incidence. There is also an emergence of pediatric IBD in regions where it had not been reported in the past (8).

The mode of delivery influences the development of the intestinal microbiota. However, meta-analyses have not confirmed a significant link between cesarean section and an increased risk of developing IBD (14,15). In contrast, systematic reviews show that breastfeeding protects against pediatric IBD, with a dose-dependent effect (16). Living in an urban environment and high ambient air pollution is associated with an increased risk of developing IBD, varying by age. Children who live close to a park or in a wooded environment have a decreased risk of developing IBD (13). Although smoking is an established risk factor in adults, no association has been reported between passive smoking or prenatal tobacco exposure and the occurrence of pediatric IBD (17). The use of antibiotics in the first years of life increases the risk of later developing Crohn's disease (18). A meta-analysis including 11 studies did not find evidence supporting a link between IBD and vaccinations, in particular the measles vaccine and BCG (19). The westernization of diets, notably in low- and middle-income countries, appears to contribute to the rising incidence of IBD, although observational studies are often limited by confounding factors. A large prospective European study found an association between red meat consumption and the risk of ulcerative colitis (HR 1.40) (20). Results regarding fat intake remain heterogeneous. In the EPIC study, higher consumption of animal fats was associated with an increased risk of UC (21). Conversely, increased consumption of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids showed a protective effect against UC (22). Clinical
In pediatric CD, only 25% of patients present the classic triad: abdominal pain, diarrhea and weight loss (9). CD can present with vague abdominal pain, unexplained anemia, recurrent fever, weight loss or growth retardation. Pediatric UC often presents with severe symptoms: profuse and nocturnal diarrhea, often mucoid and bloody, and weight loss. In more than 10% of pediatric cases, the distinction between CD and UC is difficult. The disease is then identified as indeterminate colitis. During disease progression, some cases

In more than 10% of pediatric cases, the distinction between Crohn’s disease (CD) and ulcerative colitis (UC) is difficult. The disease is then identified as indeterminate colitis. During disease progression, some cases of indeterminate colitis may evolve into CD or UC (26).

Ano-perineal lesions of CD are common in children. Examination of the anal margin must be part of the clinical examination. Rapid recognition of patients with a high suspicion of IBD for prompt endoscopic evaluation is essential (27); the presence of perineal lesions such as: ulcer, fistula or abscess can be a sufficient reason to perform an endoscopic assessment regardless of other inflammatory markers (28). Other minor criteria may be present: chronic abdominal pain (> 4 weeks), diarrhea, IBD confirmed in a first-degree relative, involuntary weight loss, presence of extra-digestive manifestations (29-31). Evaluation of growth and puberty curves is essential to consider the diagnosis, particularly in CD (32,33). (Figure 2)

Extra-digestive manifestations may precede the

Monitoring of growth and puberty curves is essential to suggest the diagnosis, particularly in CD (32,33). (Figure 2)

Extra-digestive manifestations may precede intestinal symptoms and are present at diagnosis in about 5 to 25% of cases (34). They are dominated by joint involvement, followed by dermatological involvement. Eye involvement is less common, but should also be taken into account in the initial clinical assessment. Finally, liver involvement may also be present (PSC and autoimmune hepatitis) (34). Often subtle, a thorough clinical examination and blood tests including a complete liver panel are required. An ophthalmologic examination and a dermatologic examination are recommended at the diagnosis of pediatric IBD.

Biology
The initial biological workup should include a complete blood count,
at least two inflammatory markers as well as albumin,
transaminases and gamma-GT. Data from pediatric IBD registries indicate that at diagnosis,
54% of children with mild UC and 21% of children with mild CD have
normal results for hemoglobin, albumin, CRP and ESR (35). ASCA and p-ANCA
have good specificity in children (85 to 95% depending on the studies) but low sensitivity (20 to 60%) (36).
They are not systematically performed in routine practice. Fecal calprotectin is superior to any other blood marker
for detecting intestinal inflammation.
A meta-analysis of 8 pediatric studies showed an excellent sensitivity (98%) of fecal calprotectin measurement for the diagnosis of IBD and a more modest specificity (68%) (37). Adult reference ranges can
be used in children over 4 years of age. A fecal calprotectin level above 250 μg/g of stool in a child followed for IBD is a strong argument in favor of an inflammatio

Can be used in children older than 4 years. A fecal calprotectin level above 250 μg/g of stool in a child being followed for IBD is a strong argument in favor of residual mucosal inflammation (37). In children as in adults, a pre-therapeutic workup should be performed, which includes screening for current infection—particularly tuberculosis—or assessing EBV immunization status. In pediatrics, assessment of immunity to VZV is particularly important. In cases of very early-onset IBD, an immunological workup and an immunology consultation should be carried out.
Imaging
Abdominal ultrasound and MR enterography constitute the best imaging protocol in children. Ultrasound in particular allows searching for thickening of the bowel walls, as well as mesenteric lipomatosis and/or lymphadenopathy (38,39). MR enterography has the advantage of avoiding the use of ionizing radiation to which children are much more sensitive. Linear growth and pubertal development
Short stature, defined as a height below the 3rd percentile or −2 SD, is more frequent when the disease starts early in life, with no link to the location or extent of the inflammatory lesions. In the EPIMAD cohort of 261 children, 10% had a height < −2 SD at diagnosis, compared with 7% at follow-up (49). Growth slowing is detectable in nearly 90% of children at diagnosis, and precedes digestive symptoms in half of the cases.

Screening is based on dynamic criteria: an annual growth of less than 4–5 cm from age 3 up to puberty, or a drop of one SD on the height curve. The therapeutic goal is to reach a final height close to the genetic target, calculated as follows: Girl: [Father’s height + Mother’s height - 13]/2, Boy: [Father’s height + Mother’s height + 13]/2. The expected adult height should ideally be within ±6 cm of this target.
An X‑ray of the left wrist allows assessment of bone age, which is essential in cases of growth delay. If growth is severely affected by an uncontrolled chronic disease, growth hormone therapy may be considered. Inflammation can impair bone mineralization from the time of diagnosis. This will be assessed by bone densitometry.
Pubertal development is evaluated according to Tanner stages (I to V). The absence of signs at 13 years in girls (breast development) or at 14 years in boys (increase in testicular volume) defines delayed puberty.

Cancer
Patients with IBD beginning in childhood have an increased risk of cancer compared with the general population. A meta-analysis of 66 studies including more than 38,000 children with IBD reported a standardized incidence ratio (SIR) of 2.39 (p < 0.0001; 95% CI: 2.00-2.86)50, mainly related to colorectal cancers and hematologic malignancies. Another meta-analysis estimated the relative risk at 2.46 (95% CI: 2.06-2.93), with a risk of 2.03 for CD (95% CI: 1.67-2.46) and 2.61 for UC (95% CI: 2.00-3.40) (51). Because of this increased risk, specific surveillance is recommended after 10 years of disease duration.
Transition
Transfer of care occurs when a patient, together with their medical record, moves from one healthcare professional to another at a specific point in time. In contrast, transition of care is an evolving process. It involves the “intentional and planned movement of adolescents and young adults with chronic physical and medical conditions from a child-centered care system of...

An evolving process. It involves the "intentional and planned transfer of adolescents and young adults with chronic physical and medical conditions from a child-centered care system to an adult-oriented care system" (52). During this process, adolescents learn to take responsibility for all aspects of their health themselves, become informed users of the adult healthcare system, and acquire skills for independent living and self-management of their condition (53,54). These skills include, in particular, effective communication, decision-making, self-care, self-advocacy and self-determination (55,56).

During this transition, responsibility gradually shifts from the parents to the patient. Thus, the transition of care involves a gradual change in knowledge, attitudes and behaviors (57). The transfer of care, from the pediatric gastroenterologist to the adult gastroenterologist, often represents the final stage of the transition process. Induction therapy
Exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN)
EEN, consisting of exclusive feeding with a liquid nutrition formula for 6 to 8 weeks, is recommended as first-line therapy in children with active luminal Crohn’s disease at low or moderate risk. Meta-analyses have shown similar efficacy to corticosteroids for achieving clinical remission (63,64). However, two pediatric randomized trials demonstrated a superior benefit for mucosal healing (89% with EEN versus 17% with corticosteroids, p<0.01) (65,66). The main limitations lie in acceptability (taste of the formula and/or tolerance of the nasogastric tube) and the risk of discontinuation.
In practice, efficacy is independent of the type of formula (elemental vs. polymeric) or the route of administration (oral vs. nasogastric tube) (63,67). In practice, psychological and dietary support is strongly recommended (68).
Corticosteroids

Corticosteroids
When EEN is not feasible or after failure of the latter (2 to 4 weeks), systemic corticosteroids constitute a preferred alternative. The initial dose of prednisolone depends on body weight (1 mg/kg, maximum 40 mg) and should be gradually reduced as soon as clinical remission is achieved, at the latest 4 weeks after the start of treatment. In the case of mild ileocecal involvement [L1], if EEN is insufficiently effective, ileal-release budesonide is preferable to prednisolone. In patients over 40 kg, the initial dose of budesonide is 9 mg once daily for 6 weeks, then reduced as follows: 6 mg once daily for 2 weeks, then 3 mg once daily for 2 weeks. for 2 weeks. Budesonide is recommended for localized ileocecal involvement (69,70). The use of corticosteroids should remain limited, given the adverse effects (Cushing syndrome, growth retardation, increased infection risk) (71-73).

Anti-TNF biotherapies
Anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab) are the induction treatment of choice in high-risk patients. They are also indicated after failure of immunomodulators. Pediatric data come notably from the REACH (infliximab) (74) and IMAgINE (adalimumab) (75) trials. The prospective RISK study showed that early anti-TNF treatment reduced progression to the penetrating phenotype (B3) (76). The TISKIDS trial confirmed the superiority of a "top-down" strategy with infliximab: 41% clinical remission at 52 weeks versus 12% with conservative treatment (p = 0.002) (77,78).

Infliximab: Induction is given at 5 mg/kg at weeks 0, 2 and 6, then maintenance every 8 weeks. Children < 30 kg, those with hypoalbuminemia or with extensive disease often require higher doses or shorter intervals (79).

Adalimumab: > 40 kg: 160 mg at week 0, 80 mg

at 8 weeks. Children < 30 kg, with hypoalbuminemia or with extensive disease often require higher doses or shorter intervals (79).
Adalimumab: > 40 kg: 160 mg S0, 80 mg S2, then 40 mg every 2 weeks; < 40 kg: 80/40/20 mg every 2 weeks. Given data on underdosing in young children, higher doses may be necessary in some cases.
Other options
Treatment with thalidomide can induce remission in refractory children, with high rates of mucosal healing at 12 months (80,81). However, its adverse effects (sedation, peripheral neuropathy, teratogenicity) limit its use to complex situations (82).
Maintenance therapies for remission
Methotrexate (MTX)
MTX is recommended as first-line maintenance immunosuppressant or in case of failure/intolerance to thiopurines. Administered subcutaneously, intramuscularly or orally on a weekly basis at a dose of 15 mg/m² (max 25 mg), it achieves clinical remission in 25–53% of cases at 12 months (83,84). Oral administration of folates [5 mg, 24 to 72 hours after methotrexate

weekly administration at a dose of 15 mg/m² (max 25 mg), it achieves clinical remission in 25–53% of cases at 12 months (83,84). Oral administration of folates [5 mg, 24 to 72 hours after methotrexate once weekly, or 1 mg once daily for 5 days per week] is recommended to reduce hepatotoxicity and gastrointestinal adverse effects (85). In terms of safety, the most frequent adverse effects included nausea and vomiting, elevation of liver tests, headaches, infections and hematologic toxicity (84,86). In 226 children with MC, no difference was observed between oral intake and the subcutaneous route of methotrexate, whether for steroid-free remission, therapeutic escalation or adverse effects (87).

Thiopurines (AZA, 6-MP)
Thiopurines can maintain remission (88). The recommended doses are 2.0–2.5 mg/kg/day for azathioprine and 1.0–1.5 mg/kg/day for its precursor, 6-mercaptopurine. Full dosing can be initiated from the outset, without gradual increase. The increased risk of lymphomas, including lymphoma

1.0–1.5 mg/kg/day for its precursor, 6-mercaptopurine. The full dosage can be started immediately, without gradual escalation. The increased risk of lymphomas, including hepatosplenic T-cell lymphoma (HSTCL) in young men, although rare, must be explicitly discussed (89,94). Particular vigilance is required regarding primary EBV infections under thiopurines, which are associated with a risk of lymphoproliferation (95-97).

Enteral nutrition in maintenance therapy
Maintenance enteral nutrition can prolong remission after successful induction, particularly after EEN. Long-term efficacy has not been demonstrated more because of lack of adherence to this therapy in the medium term than because of treatment ineffectiveness. Its use while awaiting the initiation of another therapy is to be preferred. More recent alternatives, such as the Crohn’s Disease Exclusion Diet combined with partial nutrition, have shown promising results in terms of tolerance and maintenance of remission at 12 weeks (98). Partial or adapted dietary regimens are still considered experimental.

Strategies for the use of biologic therapies
Therapeutic schemes, recommendations for optimization and monitoring are identical to adult recommendations. In patients starting treatment with infliximab, it is recommended to use combination therapy with an immunosuppressant. The combination infliximab + immunosuppressant reduces the risk of developing anti-infliximab antibodies and increases trough levels (105). The therapeutic management of loss of response is identical to that in adults.
Biosimilars of infliximab and adalimumab
The appearance of IFX and ADA biosimilars has reduced the cost of these therapies, with efficacy and a safety profile in children similar to those of the originator products (106). There is no restriction on their use in children, whether as first- or second-line in a patient already tra

safety data in children similar to those of the originator molecules (106). There is no restriction on their use in children, whether as first- or second-line therapy in a patient already treated with IFX or ADA (107).
Use of subcutaneous infliximab
Subcutaneous infliximab does not have marketing authorization (MA) in France for patients under 18 years. However, two studies have evaluated the switch from intravenous infliximab to the subcutaneous form in children. In the first, conducted on seven patients, all remained in remission for 40 weeks without adverse effects or new antibodies, with stable serum levels (108). The second, including 21 adolescents, shows maintained remission at six months, with excellent tolerability. These studies suggest that the transition to subcutaneous infliximab is safe, effective, and well accepted, while requiring additional studies to adjust dosing in children under 50 kg (109).
Other biologic therapies
In patients who fail to achieve or maintain clinical remission on anti-TNF therapy, despite optimization of the anti-TNF dose and the use of immunomodulators, ustekinumab or vedolizu

Patients who are unable to achieve or maintain a clinical remission on anti‑TNF, despite optimization of the anti‑TNF dose and the use of immunomodulators, may be considered for ustekinumab or vedolizumab. However, most of these other biotherapies do not have marketing authorization (MA) in France for this indication. Their experience in pediatrics is limited to cases of severe IBD resistant to usual treatments, including IFX or ADA (110,111). Ustekinumab has MA only for Crohn’s disease and for children over 40 kg. Risankizumab does not have MA in pediatrics.
For ustekinumab, the first dose is generally administered intravenously and is 6 mg/kg. Subcutaneous administration begins at week 8; adult patients receive a 90 mg injection. Children should receive a dose adjusted according to body surface area [calculated based on a reference adult with a body surface area of 1.73 m2] every 8 weeks.

For vedolizumab, a study of two multicenter pediatric cohorts of 44 and 65 children with IBD showed that respectively 41% and 32% of them were in remission at week 14 of vedolizumab treatment (112,113). None of the adverse events observed was considered severe. In patients ≥ 40 kg, vedolizumab should be administered intravenously at 300 mg, in three induction doses over 6 weeks [weeks 0-2-6], followed by a maintenance treatment of 300 mg every 8 weeks. There are no specific recommendations regarding pediatric dosing. Younger pediatric patients may require an individualized dose of 6 mg/kg up to a maximum of 300 mg, or a dose based on body surface area [calculated based on a reference adult with a body surface area of 1.73m2]. Response to vedolizumab can take time [≥ 16 weeks]. Some centers prescribe oral corticosteroids as a "bridging" treatment while waiting for vedolizumab's effects to manifest (114,115).

The use as first-line o

Some centers prescribe oral corticosteroids as a "bridging treatment" while waiting for the effects of vedolizumab to appear (114,115).
Combination therapy
The use of combination therapy as first-line treatment or in the case of therapeutic failure is not recommended in children. A recent literature review and a multicenter study conducted under the aegis of ESPGHAN do not allow conclusions on its use (116,117). Its exceptional use in pediatrics should result from a favorable opinion in a multidisciplinary consultation meeting.
Janus Kinase inhibitor
Oral Janus kinase inhibitors do not have marketing authorization for pediatric Crohn's disease. However, a recent clinical study shows that upadacitinib is effective, without new safety signals, in a small subgroup of patients with IBD (aged 9 to 20 years) (118). Its exceptional use in pediatrics should result from a favorable opinion in a multidisciplinary consultation meeting.

Surgical treatment (119)
Surgery is indicated only in cases of complications such as strictures, abscesses, fistulas or perforations, always aiming to limit the length of resected segments (120). The incidence of surgical interventions is higher in children. Preoperative nutritional optimization, whether exclusive enteral or parenteral, is essential and reduces the risk of complications, promotes better recovery and can delay or lessen the extent of the surgical procedure. EEN has been shown to reduce postoperative length of stay.

Severe acute colitis (SAC) and the role of surgery
Acute severe colitis (ASC) occurs in 10 to 15% of children with ulcerative colitis (UC) and represents a medical emergency. It is defined by a PUCAI ≥ 65. Mortality has decreased considerably (<1% today), but the risk of colectomy remains high. The medical management of severe acute colitis in pediatrics is identical to that in adults.
As in adults, when the colitis is extensive and resistant to medical treatments, when it jeopardizes vital prognosis, or in the event of dysplasia, refractory severe acute colitis, or toxic megacolon, colectomy is required. Reported pediatric rates reach 30 to 40% after 10 years of follow-up (10). However, some studies report lower frequencies, suggesting variability according to the cohorts studied and the therapeutic strategies employed (8,11,12).
Monitoring and follow-up
A close follow-up

Some studies report lower frequencies, suggesting variability according to the cohorts studied and the therapeutic strategies used (8,11,12).
Monitoring and follow-up
Close follow-up is essential, including clinical monitoring (growth in height and weight, puberty, disease activity), biological monitoring (CBC, transaminases, CRP, fecal calprotectin) and endoscopic follow-up. In the long term, the increased risk of colorectal cancer requires regular endoscopic surveillance after 8-10 years of disease, particularly in cases of pancolitis.

Conclusion
Overall, therapeutic goals and means remain comparable between children and adults. Nevertheless, in pediatric Crohn disease one should emphasize its deleterious impact on growth in stature, weight gain and pubertal maturation, parameters that are essential for monitoring inflammatory activity and for therapeutic evaluation. The diagnostic strategy therefore more readily takes into account the invasive or cumulative burden of additional tests. From a therapeutic perspective, exclusive enteral nutrition is a treatment of choice in pediatrics. It is also noted that pediatric studies on treatment are markedly fewer than those carried out in adult populations. As a result, certain drugs are not accessible due to a lack of marketing authorization for pediatric indications. Transition to adult care is a major step in the medical journey of these patients. It must be prepared and carried out with rigor.

The five key points
●	Assessment of linear growth, weight and pubertal development is an integral part of the diagnostic process and of monitoring the therapeutic response.
●	Very early-onset IBD appearing before the age of 6 years requires screening for an immunodeficiency and investigation for a monogenic cause.
●	Requesting additional tests, notably radiological ones, requires consideration of the invasiveness of the examinations and of their repetition over time.
●	Exclusive enteral nutrition is a preferred induction treatment in pediatric Crohn disease. Many therapeutic alternatives for IBD do not have marketing authorization in pediatrics. Each drug must be used optimally and for the appropriate indication before considering a therapeutic switch.
●	The pediatric-to-adult transition is a major step in patients’ medical pathway, the culmination of anticipatory and rigorous preparation.

Is your child affected by Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis? It's normal to have questions! In this sheet we sort fact from fiction and give you some tips to help your child live well despite the illness.
IS THERE A DIFFERENCE BETWEEN IBD IN CHILDREN AND ADULTS?
There is no difference between ulcerative colitis in children and in adults. But treatments must be carefully adapted, particularly regarding the use of corticosteroids, which should always be short-term because of their impact on growth.
The biggest difference concerning Crohn's disease between children and adults is the effect on growth in stature and weight and on puberty, which can be slowed or stopped. For this reason, pediatricians and surgeons attach great importance to treatment choices for this disease in children.
Although the medications used are the same as for adults (5-Aminosalicylic acids, Corticosteroids, Azathiopri

Particular importance is given to the choice of treatments in this disease in children.
Although the medications used are the same as for adults (5-aminosalicylic acids, corticosteroids, azathioprine, methotrexate, anti-TNF…), the way they are used is different. For example, cortisone (Cortancyl®) must be given as a short treatment and never for a prolonged period because of its effects on linear growth. This is why, in the pediatric age, Crohn’s disease is often treated by enteral nutrition methods that have proven their effectiveness, not only in controlling disease flares, but also on linear growth.
WHAT ARE THE FIRST SIGNS OF CROHN’S DISEASE OR ULCERATIVE COLITIS IN CHILDREN?
Diagnosis is often rapid for ulcerative colitis when there is bloody diarrhea without an infectious cause.
By contrast, in Crohn’s disease it is more often a matter of subtle signs and the diagnosis is difficult. Abdominal pain is common and occurs before diarrhea. In others, diarrhea is the first sign and

These are more often minor signs, and the diagnosis is difficult. Abdominal pain is common and occurs before diarrhea. In others, diarrhea is the first sign and is accompanied by cramps, especially after meals.
At first, psychological reasons or school refusal are often cited. Rarely the child complains of joint pain or fever without digestive disturbance. Involvement of the anus is highly suggestive in the child because it is unusual at that age (fissure, abscess).
Finally, months or years before other signs appear, the child may fail to gain weight or even lose weight and stop growing.
AT WHAT AGE DO THESE DISEASES BEGIN?
There are approximately 250,000 cases of chronic inflammatory bowel diseases (IBD) in France, and 15 to 20% occur in the pediatric age group, rarely before the age of 10. The pediatric incidence is 6 to 7 cases per 100,000 children and parents. The highest frequency of occurrence of these diseases is in young adults between 17 and 25 years.

WHAT OTHER IMPACTS
CAN IBD HAVE?
The complications of ulcerative colitis are the same as in adults: gastrointestinal bleeding with anemia, severe colitis resistant to medical treatment, perforation. These complications are rare but can sometimes lead to surgery, which—unlike Crohn's disease—can be curative (proctocolectomy) or to subtotal colectomy with preservation of the rectum, which can be treated locally with enemas.
Crohn's disease, apart from growth delay, can cause the digestive and extra-digestive signs described above. Digestive complications are dominated by narrowings (strictures) of the intestine and fistulas that connect two segments of intestine or the intestine to a neighboring organ (skin). Extra-digestive manifestations can include joint involvement, most often benign, as well as skin, eye, or liver involvement. All these manifestations are generally not serious.

HOW CAN I TELL IF MY CHILD IS GROWING PROPERLY?
A delay in height growth in a child with RCH is very rare.
On the other hand, it is common in Crohn's disease (15 to 30%). This appears as insufficient height growth. The child may notice that he or she is the smallest in their class or parents may notice that their clothes do not become too tight.
Your pediatrician should plot the growth chart and thus notice any abnormalities. Watchful parents are often the first to notice a slowing of growth or the absence of the onset of puberty. Growth delay also appears on bone X-rays.
HOW CAN THIS GROWTH DELAY BE EXPLAINED?
It is known that in these cases, growth hormones are normal. In fact, the child often eats less than would be necessary for their needs because they are not hungry. In addition, eating can lead to stomach pains, nausea

Corticosteroids can also stop growth, especially when they are given for a long time and even if they are given every other day. The effects of corticosteroids and of the disease on growth are difficult to distinguish; that is why the best treatment with the fewest side effects must be chosen carefully.

WHAT IS THE IMPACT OF SURGERY ON GROWTH ?
Surgery is necessary when there is a complication or in severe forms but does not by itself enable growth. It is often followed by an improvement that allows growth to resume.
WILL MY CHILD HAVE A NORMAL PUBERTY ?
Puberty is sometimes delayed by one or two years because of the disease, but in general it occurs normally. Children who are delayed can continue to grow to reach a normal height when they are in remission. Ultimately, your child will be a normal adult who can have a family like anyone else.
HOW CAN I HELP MY CHILD COPE WITH IBD ?
The first thing is to accept it yourself. Try to see things realistically, so your child will not feel obliged to show you that he is really ill. Get as much information as possible from your specialist. Ask as many questions as you wish and encourage your child to ask them. Some signs can be frightening for very young children, for example bloody stools… Being able to explain them is reassuring, so q

if you wish, and encourage your child to ask them. Some signs can be frightening for very young children, for example bloody stools… Being able to explain things to them is reassuring, whereas feeling helpless or ignorant is not helpful. Because of this chronic illness, you will probably spend more time with the sick child than with the others, but be sure to set aside time for them so as not to make them jealous. Try to avoid letting the illness become the centre of attention for the whole family. That’s easier said than done.
WHAT FOODS OR VITAMINS ARE USEFUL?
For some children, the disease causes deficiencies in vitamins or minerals (folates, calcium or iron…). Your doctor knows about these deficiencies and can prescribe supplements. The most important thing for the child is to eat enough: so let them eat what they like. This is not easy, for example when dairy always gives them a stomach ache or diarrhoea; you will need to find a replacement food because it is a useful source of calories and calcium. There are supplementary foods that are flavored liquids

If there are stomach problems or diarrhea, it will be necessary to find a replacement food because it is a useful source of calories and calcium. There are supplemental foods that are flavored, nutritionally balanced liquids. They are better tolerated.

In Crohn’s disease your doctor may also use a continuous tube-feeding method: the food is delivered through a small tube that passes through the nose into the stomach. This can be done at home without stopping school after a hospital stay to learn. This treatment (enteral nutrition) helps control disease flares and supports growth.

WHAT TO SAY OR NOT SAY TO MY CHILD?
As with sex education, a child hears what their maturity allows them to hear. The child will let you know. Do not shy away and encourage them to ask questions. The pediatrician will care for them better if they can have a dialogue with the child. Many children participate better once they have understood. A little girl told her mother that she had accepted being operated on without fear because she had been able to talk about it openly and understand what was going to happen.
Older children and adolescents have the right to know about this condition that affects their bodies and changes them. Conversely, it is not useful to explain all the possible complications of the disease to a young child.
WHAT TO DO IF MY CHILD IS TOO SICK TO EAT?
When a child is too sick to eat, they can receive all the necessary nutrients by an infusion into a large vein. This is called parenteral nutrition (PN), exclusive parenteral nutrition (EPN) when they take nothing by mouth.
A supplement can also be given through the veins in addition to a normal diet. PN

what is called parenteral nutrition (PN), exclusive (EPN) when they are no longer taking anything by mouth.
A supplement can also be given through the veins in addition to a normal diet. PN can last weeks or months, if necessary in the hospital or at home, for the time needed to rest the digestive tract or to improve their general condition. PN can also “prepare” a child for surgery in this way. Caloric intake is markedly increased and the majority of children begin to grow again during this intravenous feeding.
WHAT PROBLEMS ARE ENCOUNTERED WITH A SICK ADOLESCENT?
Adolescents are very sensitive about their physical appearance, and inflammatory diseases or their treatment can have consequences they find hard to live with. They often feel upset, grumpy and depressed, or oppositional during flare-ups.
They are deeply affected by changes in their weight or facial appearance because of the treatment or the disease. They may therefore try to miss appointments, ignore symptoms to avoid being given medications. They may be afraid of surgery and ashamed of it

of the illness. They will therefore try to miss appointments, ignore symptoms to avoid being given medication. They may be afraid of surgery and ashamed to say so or to ask what the operation will be like. For some of them, short stature constantly reminds them of their illness even when they are doing well. It is the sign they suffer from most often.
To help them, the pediatrician works with a team that includes at least one psychologist and a social worker.
WHAT SHOULD I DO IF MY CHILD IS DEPRESSED?
Your child may become depressed during a relapse when they realize that the illness can come back. Their change in behavior may also be due to the treatment (Prednisone or others). Explain to them that it does not last, your doctor will be able to help because the symptoms are linked to the flare-up and the child returns to normal activity as soon as improvement occurs.
ARE THERE THINGS I SHOULD NOT DO?
As with all chronic illnesses, parents tend to overprotect their children ("helicopter parents").
It is natural and it often helps them to feel well; but as soon as your daughter or your

As with all chronic illnesses, parents tend to overprotect their children ("helicopter parents").
This is natural and often helps them feel well; but as soon as your daughter or your son is old enough, it is important that they learn to take care of themselves, to be independent, to manage their own treatment, or to call the doctor directly when they need to. This illness can lead to tensions between parents and children, especially during adolescence.
Parents' worry shows itself when the child does not eat or loses weight; you should then talk with them because they may need to discuss it. This discussion can also take place with your doctor.

CAN THEY DO SPORTS OR ATTEND SCHOOL NORMALLY?
Children can do what they want and are able to undertake, except if, during a flare-up, the activity causes significant fatigue (competition), worsens digestive symptoms or joint pain. Sometimes the sports program must be adapted to the child’s condition. Children quickly learn to tolerate a certain level of pain or discomfort and live with it. They also know when it becomes unbearable.
You can give them the responsibility of deciding whether they are able to go to class. Forcing a child to go to class, or conversely to stay in bed, is not a solution and leads to conflicts. They should also be encouraged to find practical solutions to school problems: know where the toilets and the school nurse are, and bring spare clothes if needed.
SHOULD I INFORM HIS/HER TEACHERS AND CLASSMATES?

SHOULD I INFORM THEIR TEACHERS AND CLASSMATES?
It is up to your child to decide. Because this illness is hard to explain and children can sometimes be cruel to one another, your child may want to protect themself by saying nothing. Respect their choice.
Of course, if hospitalizations or absences are frequent, you should inform the teachers or the principals. Your child should also be allowed to leave the classroom in the middle of a lesson when they need to, or to have their schoolwork brought to them when they miss a lot.
WHAT IS THE RISK FOR MY OTHER CHILDREN OF HAVING THE SAME ILLNESS?
It is possible, although uncommon, that a family member has the same disease. Studies have shown that about 15 to 20% of patients have a relative in their family with IBD. Even though we now know that there are genetic, sometimes hereditary factors in these diseases, that is not sufficient, and there are necessary environmental factors that are not yet identified; it is currently impossible to predict who may have it, and as

But this is not sufficient, and there are environmental factors that are essential but not yet identified; it is currently impossible to predict who may develop it, and because the risk is low, no doctor discourages parents from having more children when there is already someone affected by IBD.
IN SUMMARY
WHAT NOT TO DO :
1/ Overprotect them (wrap them in cotton wool) and unduly forbid them from activities or projects on the pretext that this may worsen the disease: this is incorrect and the vast majority of children with IBD can and should lead a normal life. No career, even those involving responsibility and "stress", is off-limits to them and precise studies show that the professional level of children with IBD is normal. Experience also shows that many patients with IBD are "fighters".
2/ Unnecessarily forbid them certain activities or foods except on medical advice.
3/ Conversely, act as if the disease does not exist and delay medical consultation.
The most appropriate attitude :
A discreet but vigilant approach.

What is the CDED ?
The exclusion of certain foods, replaced by artificial nutrition (oral or by tube), could allow induction of remission and healing in Crohn’s disease1. Exclusive Enteral Nutrition (EEN) is nevertheless difficult to follow for several weeks.
The Crohn’s Disease Exclusion Diet (CDED), or the exclusion diet for Crohn’s disease, is a therapeutic dietary strategy based on the exclusion of certain foods, replaced p

Crohn’s Disease Exclusion Diet (CDED), or exclusion diet in Crohn’s disease, is a therapeutic dietary strategy based on the exclusion of certain foods, replaced by specific foods combined with an enteral nutrition solution. It is an alternative to EEN1,2.
Its effectiveness has been demonstrated by international studies and recognized in practice since 2019, first in children, then in adults2.
What could CDED offer me?
Clinical remission: a study*3 demonstrated about 80% remission at 6 weeks in selected patients (mild to moderate Crohn’s disease in children), and among them, some showed improvement as early as 3 weeks3,4.
Better tolerance: unlike EEN, CDED allows an increasingly varied diet while avoiding pro-inflammatory foods3,4.
A positive impact on the gut microbiota: by limiting exposure to certain additives and ultra-processed foods, the diet promotes a protective bacterial balance3,4.
A transient effect to treat the flare: CDED is used for a defined period to stabilize

and ultra-processed foods, the diet promotes a protective bacterial balance3,4.
A transient effect to treat the flare: the CDED is used over a defined period to stabilize the disease, before progressing to a more diversified diet. In addition, the CDED can be used as a transition treatment before another therapeutic management or in the perioperative setting5.

CDED in brief
➔ The Crohn’s Disease Exclusion Diet (CDED in English) is a therapeutic dietary strategy based on the exclusion of certain foods, replaced by specific foods combined with a nutritional solution

The Crohn’s Disease Exclusion Diet (CDED) is a therapeutic dietary strategy based on the exclusion of certain foods, replaced by specific foods combined with an enteral nutrition formula.
➔  Il is divided into several phases allowing a progressive reintroduction of foods while ensuring control of inflammation.
➔  In addition to the enteral nutrition formula, 5 foods are mandatory and other foods are allowed in defined quantities and frequencies. The list of allowed foods expands as one progresses through the phases of the CDED.
➔  Follow-up with professionals trained in this diet is essential.

I have a Mickey, but what exactly is it? To move, the body needs the energy that comes from the food we eat. But first the food must be absorbed — that is the role of the digestive system. Unfortunately, yours has a chronic inflammatory disease of the intestine that we also call a Mickey. 

There are two types depending on the area affected: Crohn's disease, which can affect the whole digestive system, and ulcerative colitis, which only concerns the rectum and the colon. Because of it, you have abdominal pain, frequent diarrhea, sometimes with blood, and you lose your appetite. You can also be tired, have mouth ulcers, joint pain, a fever, or eye problems. 

These are flare-up phases of the disease that alternate with quiet phases. These symptoms are caused by inflammation in your body because your immune system is too active. Indeed, when you catch a virus or a microbe called an antigen, an alarm is given and you begin to produce soldiers called antibodies. The battle starts and your army shows no mercy — it's like fire. Up to that point, everyone works in the same way. But you, your army doesn't...

this will produce soldiers called antibodies. The battle begins and your army shows no mercy, it’s like a fire. Up to that point, everyone’s immune system works the same way. But for you, your army can’t stop fighting and this causes sores in your intestine. To put out this inflammation, you therefore need to better control your immune defenses with medications. We do not yet know the exact causes of these diseases; scientists study them to find out where they come from and thus treat them better. Maybe some anomalies in your DNA favor the appearance of the disease, or some antigens, when they infect you, trigger it. Or your lifestyle or your environment may play a role, but we don’t know which. What we do know more precisely is that your gut microbiota, which naturally contains a whole set of microorganisms like good bacteria, needs to be balanced. It is the loss of these balances, perhaps favored by ultra-processed foods, certain medications, or other factors like tobacco or pollution. We also know that it is neither contagious nor hereditary, nor caused by a single food.

In France, about 300,000 people live with this disease and 15% of them are children. Each year, roughly 8,000 new cases are diagnosed. Do you still have questions about your illness? When you have Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis, there are sometimes difficult moments. You may have pain, fatigue, or urgent needs to go to the bathroom. These symptoms can sometimes disturb your concentration in class and even force you to miss lessons. Sometimes you may feel unmotivated or stressed at the idea of catching up on missed classes. And you may be wondering whether to tell your classmates about your illness. These illnesses are rather taboo and that's understandable given the part of the body they mostly affect. It is therefore rather difficult to talk about them at school or middle school, but it is also very hard to keep it secret. When you're in class, you sometimes need to leave, which prompts questions from classmates. These unpleasant moments are often stressful. That's why you especially need to learn to relax and stay calm. In this video, we give you tips and the help that will allow you to have a stress-free schooling.

What are my rights and the possible supports?
If you have a medical condition, you have the right to an individualized care plan, or PAI. This plan makes it possible to put special accommodations in place for you, such as access to the toilet when needed; having spare clothes at the school nurse's office; having a private space to take your medication; or having meals adapted to your allergies. It also allows certain activities to be adapted if necessary, for example in PE. The PAI is a valuable support, and it is important to request it even if you feel well. If symptoms return, it will then be easier to put solutions in place quickly to make your daily life easier. You can also benefit from extra time (one-third more time) during exams. It's easier to quickly put in place solutions to make your daily life easier. You can also benefit from extra time during exams. This means that you have more time to take your tests. This can really help when you don't feel at your best or when you need to go to the toilet during the exam. For absences, usually—and with a bit of organization from your middle school or high school—your teachers or classmates will take care of giving you the missed lessons so you can catch up. If you have to miss many classes, there are arrangements such as the home educational assistance service, or sa pade. It allows you to continue following classes from home with the help of a teacher. In case of hospitalization, you can benefit from hospital school, with teachers who support you in your learning and stay connected with your school. Will my disease stop me from growing normally? With these diseases, you can be smaller and thinner than others, at least at the beginning. Eating when you're not hungry or have a stomach ache isn't easy. In addition, inflammation uses a lot of energy. So even if you eat as much as other children, it's not enough for your body. You will have trouble growing properly. But if you take your treatments correctly and follow your doctor's advice, you will reach a normal adult height. There is no particular diet; you mainly need to grow. So we let you eat what you want, according to your tolerance and what you like. But don't forget that to properly feed your microbiota, you should avoid ready-made foods, like tray meals, and eat a balanced diet. When you don't feel well, it is recommended to avoid raw vegetables and milk, because they are hard to digest. You may be offered artificial feeding to treat the disease and help you regain weight. This is called enteral nutrition. It is a mixture of liquid foods given using a tube that passes through the nose.

It's a mixture of liquid foods given using a tube that passes through the nose and goes into the stomach at night while you sleep. Later, you will need to avoid smoking, because it makes the disease worse and it is not good for your health. ... Can you explain rectocolitis — what is it? So rectocolitis is hemorrhagic; it's a kind of colitis, that is to say it's also a chronic inflammatory bowel disease. But it will affect only the colon, it will start in the rectum and extend more or less into the colon, so there will be no other involvement of the digestive tract. And it is a more superficial involvement. So it too has somewhat the same pathophysiology — the pathophysiology, that is, it's roughly the same causes. But I say they are close because there are things where the factors are not exactly the same. That is to say that in ulcerative colitis, dietary factors will still be a little less important. And the environmental factor that is different is tobacco. Because tobacco in Crohn's disease will worsen the illness, lead to treatment resistance, and surgery will be a factor of poor prognosis. Digestion is a process that already begins in the mouth with chewing.  
When we chew, enzymes in the saliva start to break down the food.  
Then we swallow into the esophagus, which pushes the food into the stomach.  
The stomach is a big pouch with lots of muscles that churn the contents to turn them into a mush.  
This mush is then pushed into the duodenum and into the small intestine.  
This intestine is very important; it measures about four meters.  
If you look at the cells, they have lots of villi on their surface to further increase absorption.  
It is in this intestine, thanks to enzymes produced by the pancreas, that food is broken down into amino acids, fatty acids and simple sugars, which are absorbed and pass into the blood.  
And thanks to that, when we are a child we grow and gain weight. As an adult, we maintain our weight.  
And once all these nutrients have arrived to nourish us, the waste is pushed into the colon.

In an adult, we maintain their weight. And then once all those riches, all those nutrients, have arrived to feed us, there will be waste that will be pushed into the colon. And with water — and the colon — it will serve to reabsorb the water so that in the end a little poop comes out, like the story of the little top.

... In remission, should I continue your follow-up visits or not? Yes, yes, yes, because as we said, these are chronic diseases, so of course you need to continue monitoring to check that everything is fine. In fact, we monitor blood tests and sometimes request fecal calprotectin follow-ups — it’s a test that measures inflammation in the stool. In pediatrics, we measure height and weight, and in adults the weight. And yes, in remission you can see the gastroenterologist once or twice a year to make sure everything is okay and that the disease isn’t back; sometimes it’s sneaky and returns without us realizing it, so you really need to make sure everything is fine. ... ...

... Does my treatment work? Treatments do work, but actually, today we don't have — and it would be great to have this — when we do the first colonoscopy and take a biopsy, to put a small chip that would tell us, well, this inflammation goes down that pathway, and his goes down this other pathway, and we could immediately give the right medicine. So today we do have medicines, we've talked about biologic therapies, which target certain inflammatory pathways, but which will work in some patients and not in others, and other ones will work in others and not in some. So indeed sometimes we will have to try several medicines. We will find the right one, but today the medicines still work pretty well; biologic therapy works well.

Well, the colonoscopy—do I need to prepare for it? Yes, because you actually need to arrive with a completely clean colon, so you'll have to drink a solution that will make you go to the toilet and empty your colon. You'll also need to follow a low-residue diet so that it's easier to evacuate. So yes, it's an exam that requires preparation.

Stress: friend or Andy?
It's more of an enemy in the sense that stress, when you're not ill, causes stomach pain and gives diarrhea. In an intestine that is a bit more fragile, it can take on somewhat larger proportions. And doctors will say that stress does not, by itself, cause it to develop. But when you listen to patients, you realize that sometimes this disease is discovered after certain episodes of greater stress. It's more of an enemy, actually.
