... Where do these diseases come from? So these are diseases that, in fact, we have known about since the late 19th century. They were initially diseases of the industrialized North and therefore have a large environmental component — with pollution, the way we eat, probably processed and industrialized foods — which in turn cause changes in the microbiota, and these microbiota changes then lead to a recruitment of the immune system that will attack the digestive tract ...

... Do you have the right to examine me and what does the law say? Yes. You do not have the right to examine someone if they do not want it, and I even think that we must ask for your consent before examining you and that you explicitly agree. After that, there are sometimes tests that we absolutely need in order to decide something or to have a precise idea, and if you do not agree and think the test is really important, we have to explain how important the test is so that you understand and can come to a decision. But I believe that if you do not agree, even if they examine you, one cannot intrude into you without your consent and that would, as they say, be counterproductive, because to move forward we actually need a therapeutic alliance, so we cannot do things against you.

... Oh, during a flare-up, what should I eat? Well, during a flare-up, sometimes some gastroenterologists recommend fiber-free diets. But that's just because fiber can, can, well, make you go to the toilet more. But in truth, there isn't really a specific diet to follow. I think you should still keep eating and... avoid foods that are nevertheless pro-inflammatory when you have a flare-up. So sodas, alcohol cause diarrhea. Things like that. ... ... ... ...

What is ETP? ETP is therapeutic patient education. The aim of therapeutic education is to make sure that patients are not at a loss about their illness and their daily life, and that they learn to live well with their condition. It is therefore about giving them all the tools so that life is easier and smoother: that they understand their disease, that they know who the resource persons are, that they understand how to anticipate flare-ups, how to react in case of flare-ups so it doesn't drag on, and how to express things easily. So therapeutic education is actually about giving power back to the patient, and not having prescriptions simply handed down in a top-down way. The patient is truly at the center of care.

What are fibers? Fibers are super important. They are found in vegetables and fruits. And actually they are not digested. They pass straight into the intestine. And that allows the gut microbiota to use them, since it can digest them. And that will give richness to the microbiota. And that's why it's so important to eat fruits and vegetables to restore this richness of the microbiota. And that diet is also very important, and that it can help play an anti-inflammatory role.

It's normal to feel sad when you have an IBD. When you suffer, when you often have diarrhea or when you think you look ugly, it's normal not to feel good. You feel alone, you think it's unfair, you cry a lot, and you have the impression that no one understands. People, even friends, don't always understand what an IBD is, and they can be awkward. If you want, you can explain your illness to them so they understand you better and can give you support if you need it. If you're feeling down, talk to your parents or your doctor; they can help or find someone who can. You can also meet and talk with other teenagers who have Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis. For example, the FAF offers family stays that allow children and their parents to meet and share their experiences. And from the age of 12, you can take part in the teen camp that happens every summer without parents, but you can bring a friend of your choice. Is it normal to be a little tired? Yes, it's completely normal to be tired with this illness because TNF, for example, is a molecule; it goes into the brain and makes you tired. So inflammation makes you tired. Pain makes you tired, so this is an inflammatory disease that causes pain and diarrhea; with diarrhea there is dehydration, and we lose iron. All of that are factors that cause tiredness. So the disease, for example, causes fatigue. But sometimes, even when someone is in remission, when they don't have anemia, when they don't have iron deficiency, when they don't have zinc deficiency, when they don't have vitamin deficiency, when they are in really deep remission, fatigue—sometimes it's the last thing that remains and patients are exhausted. And that can have many causes. The first thing is that it's not so simple to live with a chronic disease and to carry that mental load all the time, always having to anticipate everything, wondering what to do, whether I should cancel, whether I should call in sick, etc. So that mental load is tiring. There can be a fati

seeing everything to anticipate, anticipating, possibly — but what do I do then, should I rinse, should I turn the sick person, etc. So this mental load is tiring. There can also be psychological fatigue, meaning that sometimes we don't accept it. We don't accept, we don't accept that we will live with a chronic illness and it's a burden, and that burden is exhausting. And actually, there is also poor eating habits, meaning eating in any old way, because we tell ourselves, after all, if I eat without fiber, without… also without vitamins, without vitamins, without vitamins — that I would have less to heal, less stomach pain. If I'm too tired to go exercise, but in fact it's a vicious circle. Exercise will re-energize. So keeping up physical activity will give you back some pep; eating, making sure there are fruits, vegetables, dairy products, vitamin-rich foods — all that will also create an energizing effect. When the burden is too heavy, maybe you need to accept getting a little helping hand, and the mental load is the same. There comes a time, then…

Yes, it will produce an energizing effect. When the burden is too heavy, maybe you have to accept getting help from a little helping hand, and it's the same for mental load. At some point, you'll have to break the hamster wheel that keeps running in your head, because that's exhausting. Getting help and perhaps turning to sophrology techniques, yoga or other things that will break this circle of thought. So, a healthy lifestyle is adopted, plus lots of little things on the side.

Can we treat my miki? Miki are illnesses that you carry inside you, and that can turn on and off at different times. For now, it is not possible to cure them. That’s why it’s important to be well followed, especially to avoid or limit the risk of relapse of the disease. And fortunately, there are medicines that can help you feel better. When you are having a flare-up of the disease, we use medications depending on how serious your crisis is, such as the five hasards, corticohyd, immunosuppressants or biotherapies. Some medicines help put out the fire, like corticohyd. Others work more in the long term to keep the calm phases, like immunosuppressants or biotherapies. That’s why, to avoid getting sick again, you may be prescribed immunosuppressants to take daily for several years, or very targeted biotherapies against inflammation in the form of injections given at home or by infusion in the hospital. Finally, it is also possible to follow a specific diet that can be as effective as a maintenance treatment. Other medications

treatment given to you at home or by infusion in hospital. Finally, it is also possible to follow a special diet that can be as effective as a maintenance treatment. Other medicines can also calm the pain and help you heal. When medicines don't work, or your intestine is too damaged, it is sometimes necessary to have surgery. It is often very effective, even if it is not always definitive. For example, when two organs are connected to each other when they shouldn't be, which is called a fistula, or when a part of the digestive tract is narrowed, which is called a stricture (stenosis). The surgeon then removes the diseased parts during surgery. Sometimes the intestine is connected to the skin; this is called a stoma. It is generally covered by a plastic pouch. To know whether the medicines are working and whether the disease is active, you need to have medical tests regularly. Ultrasound, which allows seeing inside the body using an ultrasound probe that is placed on your belly. Fecal calprotectin, which allows examining your stools to assess the level of inflammation in your intestine.

the inside of the body using an ultrasound probe that is placed on your belly. The fecal calprotectin test, which allows us to examine your stools to assess the level of inflammation in your intestine. The blood test, during which a small sample of your blood is taken for analysis. The endoscopy, performed while you sleep, during which a tiny camera attached to a flexible tube with a light is inserted into your intestine via the mouth or the nose. And finally, the MRI or CT scan, which are large shiny machines that allow us to get very clear images of your belly without opening it. Do you still have questions about your illness? Understanding your illness and your treatment is the best way to keep your spirits up and to fight to get better.

... And research, where are we? So, research, in 20 years already, it's a croyabse that was developed as a medication to treat, to put the disease into remission. We haven't yet found what is needed to cure the disease, but research is still looking for that. There has also been research on the microbiota, since it has been said to play a role in the disease, with large laboratories that have a lot of research on the microbiota. So, yeah, there is very, very, very active research there.

... Can you explain Crohn's disease to me? The disease, when it is an inflammatory bowel disease, is a condition that causes inflammation, which can affect any segments from the mouth to the anus, but in a segmental and focal way, with lesions, then healthy parts, then lesions, then healthy parts, and lesions that are classically described as transmural, meaning they can be quite deep. It is a disease that has a genetic component, but the genetic part is not that large — it accounts for about 10% — and it will be expressed in a given environment. So the environment is classically an industrialized, northern setting, with a certain type of diet, and that will set the stage together with the microbiota, which is all the bacteria we have in the colon, which are very useful to us because usually we live in symbiosis with them; in particular, they actually provide us with vitamins. But here there will be an imbalance of this microbiota, which will be a bit disturbed and will induce a somewhat inappropriate immune reaction. So all of that will create a...

e, that for us. But here, there will be an imbalance in the microbiota that will be a bit disturbed, which will induce a somewhat inappropriate immune reaction. So all of that will create a slightly increased intestinal permeability, which means immune cells will come — they will be recruited to a place where they shouldn't be. And so they attack small pieces of the digestive tract, as I told you, in a segmental and focal way. And so it is a disease that progresses by flares interspersed with remission, which means that at some times things will be very bad, and then there will be times when it is calmer.

What are biotherapies? Biotherapies are molecules. In the body, cells talk to each other by sending chemical molecules or signaling molecules, things like cytokines. They send these and that allows them to have a dialogue between them. Biotherapies are targeted medicines; they are antibodies that will catch a cytokine or a particular molecule and neutralize it. For example, anti-TNFs— infliximab, adalimumab—are medicines, they are antibodies that will catch TNF, and this molecule, TNF, is a molecule that goes to the site of inflammation. It's like a fire truck with a huge siren: TNF is the siren and it calls, in fact, all the lymphocytes that will arrive and start to attack the digestive tissue. So the antibodies that come will catch that siren, silence it, which creates an artificial silence and therefore creates a remission.

I can stabilize it, well, that's said. Over the past 20 years, a great deal of research has been undertaken with many treatments. So it's easier today to stabilize these diseases because, indeed, we try treatments, biologic therapies that are targeted at inflammation and that can indeed produce longer periods of remission. So it is possible to stabilize the disease.

... Will I have symptoms other than digestive ones? So, in severe inflammatory diseases, there can be inflammatory manifestations that appear in other organs, and in particular the joints. Inflammatory joint involvement is a particular kind of involvement: it is pain that you can have in the morning with a feeling of not being able to use your joints, and what we call "morning stiffness," that is, during the first 30 minutes you are a bit like an old person — it hurts, it's hard to move, and then as the day goes on it gets better and better. So, that is an inflammatory involvement and it will need to be managed. Pain that comes at the end of the day is more mechanical, and that may have nothing to do with the disease, and there can also be other involvements. So when someone has bone pain, that is not normal in a child or an adult; we will see bone pain. Inflammatory diseases, in fact, cause demineralization, and that can lead to vertebral compression fractures. So if you have pain in the b...

In a child or an adult, you can see bone pain. Inflammatory bone diseases actually cause demineralization, and this can lead to vertebral compression fractures. So if you have bone pain, it’s not just because the backpack is too heavy. You should talk about it and have an X‑ray to make sure the disease hasn’t caused a vertebral compression fracture. There can also be other bone manifestations, for example in the jaw, the sternum, or the clavicle. This is called chronic recurrent multifocal osteomyelitis. If there are pains all over like that, you should also speak with a gastroenterologist; they will do routine tests to check the digestive tract. So overall, the message is to talk about it, to say something, and not to dismiss it by saying “it’s just the backpack” or “it’s nothing.”

... Are there other treatments besides infusions? Biotherapies exist by intravenous route and now there are subcutaneous routes; for example a cliquestime, which is prescribed only by intravenous route. There are subcutaneous forms, they do exist. And so, when patients are doing well and are in remission, we can offer them subcutaneous forms to use at home. It's the same for the antivio, but a subcutaneous form exists. So we have more and more treatments given subcutaneously. However, the start of treatment — a cliquestime before tivio and then the anti-IL-23 — is carried out by infusion: the first three infusions are given intravenously.

... Well, should we prepare it or not? Yes, you need to prepare the appointment, because the gastroenterologist will have lots of very important questions. So you should indeed come with the number of bowel movements you have per day, know if there is any blood, and whether they are soft. There is a question that is often taboo and that you have trouble answering: the question of urgencies. But actually, we cannot guess those symptoms; we cannot guess if you have urgencies, strong urges, or accidents. And that is really important, because actually it is not acceptable. And if it exists, you need to say it so that we can adapt the treatment. So yes, it should be prepared.

... Will I have to have surgery? In Crohn's disease, surgery is performed in case of complications. It is for lesions that are difficult to manage medically. Or in case of failure of many treatments with an inflammatory lesion that is somewhat beyond control. And so, sometimes, to protect the suture — that is, in fact, we cut out the diseased piece and then we stitch. But sometimes, to protect that suture, we have to put a stoma upstream, that is, we divert the digestive tract. This is to protect the stitch so there is no load and healing can take place as well as possible. But this stoma is, in that case, temporary and afterwards we can restore the digestive tract to its continuity. In very rare cases, sometimes, when the disease has been hard to control, when it has caused many inflammations, when there are perineal fistulas and so on that are very, very severe and difficult. In very rare cases, sometimes, we come to propose permanent stomas to manage this inflammation. We were unable to control it medically but it remains exc

difficult. In very rare cases, sometimes we end up proposing permanent stomas to manage this inflammation. We could not control it medically, but that remains exceptional. In ulcerative colitis, surgery can be necessary either because we are dealing with a severe colitis that we could not control medically and it is a rescue colectomy — we remove the colon. But today, with all the medicines we have, that is exceptional. Or it is a colon that, over time, has been repeatedly ill, not well treated and is no longer functional, and finally there are so many episodes of urgency and inflammation not controlled medically that we will perform a colectomy. And in fact, because the disease in ulcerative colitis affects only the colon, once we remove the colon there will remain a reservoir, and we will be able to reduce the treatments. On the other hand, indeed, we will replace the inflammatory disease with having less colon. And so sometimes patients will have more stools, one stool after each meal. In any case, they will need to adapt to their reservoir.

inflammatory disease affecting more of the colon. And so sometimes these are patients who will have more stools, one stool after each meal. In any case, they will need to readapt their colon, their reservoir, to the fact of having a slightly greater continence.
